title: Transition-to-Success Mentoring Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a new program where the Department of Education gives money to schools and other groups to start mentoring programs for middle school students who need extra help. These programs pair students with trained adults called success coaches who meet with them regularly to help them get ready for high school. The coaches help students set goals and give them support to succeed in school and life. The Department of Education will also work with other government offices to make sure these programs have good resources and can help students with disabilities or students coming back from correctional facilities.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Transition-to-Success Mentoring Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Transition-to-Success Mentoring Program
sections.text:
This section changes an existing education law by adding a new program. The Secretary of Education will give grants (money) to qualified organizations to create, grow, or support mentoring programs in schools. These programs help at-risk middle school students move successfully from middle school to high school.

To get a grant, organizations must apply to the Secretary. The Secretary will give priority to organizations that serve children with the greatest needs, especially those in poor neighborhoods, high-crime areas, or rural areas, or who attend schools with lots of community violence. Priority also goes to programs that help students prepare for college or careers through job training, internships, and visits to colleges. The Secretary prefers programs where mentors and students work together for at least one school year and where children helped design the program.

Organizations that receive grants must use the money to create mentoring programs in all their middle schools. Every eligible student gets a success coach (a mentor) who creates a success plan with the student, their parents, and school staff. This plan includes the student's goals for school, personal life, college, and career, plus ways to reach these goals. The success coach must meet with the student at least once a month to help them succeed and at least four times a year with parents and teachers to check progress.

Grant money can also pay for training success coaches about trauma, homelessness, foster care, cultural differences, and working with students who have disabilities. It can pay for field trips, career activities, college visits, and program evaluation.

Grants last up to five years. Organizations must report each year about how many students participated, their backgrounds, their academic success, attendance, and other outcomes. The Secretary must give Congress reports after three years and five years about how well these programs work.

The Secretary will work with the Office of Juvenile Justice to help grant recipients get resources and training. The section defines key terms like eligible entity (school districts or partnerships with community organizations), eligible student (at-risk middle school students), and success coach (trained mentors who work in schools).
